coredata.dc:title: Solubility tuning of alkyl amine functionalized carbon quantum dots for selective detection of nitroexplosive
coredata.dc:creator: Mondal, Tapas Kumar
Kapuria, Arijit
Miah, Milon
Saha, Shyamal K.
coredata.dc:description: The major issue of luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is the solubility in wide range of solvent and disappearance of luminescence behavior in its solid state due to aggregation caused quenching. To resolve this issue, in the present work, we have tuned the solubility of carbon quantum dots in different kinds of solvents through surface functionalization technique. Here, we have synthesized three sets of alkyl amine functionalized carbon quantum dots from citric acid and alkyl amine by solvothermal method. We have observed that the solubility of CQDs increases from aqueous medium to organic medium with increasing length of hydrophobic alkyl chains attached at the surface of CQDs. It is seen that n-butyl amine functionalized CQDs (C4-CQD) are soluble in any type of solvents. Here we have also observed that the C4-CQD shows aggregation induced emission instead of aggregation caused quenching in solid state and shows bright yellow luminescence color upon excitation. Besides this, we also report selective detection of both dinitroaniline (DNA) and dinitrophenol (DNP) using this C4-CQD via fluorescence quenching and colorimetric methods. In organic medium both shows significant quenching with emission peak of carbon quantum dots is red shifted (∼30 nm) in presence of DNP whereas no significant peak shift is observed for DNA. CQDs containing paper strip shows yellow spot in presence of DNP but no such characteristic color is appeared after addition of DNA. Using these two techniques, we have selectively identified both DNA and DNP among other nitro compounds by our as-synthesized carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Solubility tuning of alkyl amine functionalized carbon quantum dots for selective detection of nitroexplosive

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Sciences, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Jadavpur, Kolkata, 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Sciences, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science Jadavpur, Kolkata, 700032, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The major issue of luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is the solubility in wide range of solvent and disappearance of luminescence behavior in its solid state due to aggregation caused quenching. To resolve this issue, in the present work, we have tuned the solubility of carbon quantum dots in different kinds of solvents through surface functionalization technique. Here, we have synthesized three sets of alkyl amine functionalized carbon quantum dots from citric acid and alkyl amine by solvothermal method. We have observed that the solubility of CQDs increases from aqueous medium to organic medium with increasing length of hydrophobic alkyl chains attached at the surface of CQDs. It is seen that n-butyl amine functionalized CQDs (C4-CQD) are soluble in any type of solvents. Here we have also observed that the C4-CQD shows aggregation induced emission instead of aggregation caused quenching in solid state and shows bright yellow luminescence color upon excitation. Besides this, we also report selective detection of both dinitroaniline (DNA) and dinitrophenol (DNP) using this C4-CQD via fluorescence quenching and colorimetric methods. In organic medium both shows significant quenching with emission peak of carbon quantum dots is red shifted (∼30 nm) in presence of DNP whereas no significant peak shift is observed for DNA. CQDs containing paper strip shows yellow spot in presence of DNP but no such characteristic color is appeared after addition of DNA. Using these two techniques, we have selectively identified both DNA and DNP among other nitro compounds by our as-synthesized carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are new emerging carbon based nanomaterials having tunable optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent photostability [1]. Due to these unique properties CQDs have potential applications in the field of bio medical [2,3], bio-imaging [4–6], sensing [7–11], optoelectronics [12,13], electrocatalysis [14–18], photo catalysis [19–22], and energy storage devices [23–25]. A typical carbon quantum dot has carbogenic core with some surface passivated functional groups. Till date extensive research has been carried out on carbon-based quantum dots especially, tuning of band gap, optical property and catalytic activities. Most of the carbon quantum dots are water soluble due to presence of hydrophilic surface functional groups. Due to water solubility and biocompatibility, it is wieldy used in bio-imaging, drug delivery, and sensing applications. But non-polar organic solvent soluble carbon quantum dots are required for making LED and photo voltaic devices. As the water-soluble CQDs are hard to soluble in organic medium, some research groups synthesized carbon quantum dots which are selectively soluble only in organic medium. Solubility of CQDs is very important factor for different types of applications. Wu et al. synthesized organic soluble carbon quantum dots from 1, 3, 6-trinitropyrene in toluene medium via solvothermal methods for LED applications [26]. Their synthesized carbon dots are soluble in non-polar solvent as well as polar solvents except water due to presence of hydrophobic graphitic core with nitro groups at the edge sites. Panniello et al. synthesized oil soluble carbon dots from anhydrous citric acid in non-coordinating solvent hexadecyl amine by hot injecting method. This oil soluble quantum dot shows white luminescence and used them for the fabrication of white LED [27]. Generally, two kinds of carbon quantum dots are required either it is soluble in aqueous medium or organic medium based on their purpose of applications. CQDs that are soluble in wide range of solvent are highly needed. Although, large number of research articles are available in the literature of water-soluble carbon quantum dots and on organic soluble carbon quantum dots however research articles on both aqueous and organic soluble carbon quantum dots are relatively unexplored. Therefore, solubility tuning of CQDs in different kinds of solvents including polar protic, polar aprotic and non-polar solvents is indeed an interesting area of research.

Usually in the literature, most of the carbon quantum dots show luminescence properties in solution state and luminescence disappears in its solid form or film due to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [28,29]. This ACQ of carbon quantum dots in solid state hinders practical applications in different areas via. optoelectronic devices and fingerprint identification where CQDs are basically used in its solid form. Therefore, it is essential to prevent the aggregation caused quenching behavior of CQDs for better performance in light emitting devices. Some techniques are reported to prevent the ACQ of carbon quantum dots in solid state by making a composite with polymer or silica matrix [30–33]. Presently, PL quantum efficiency of carbon quantum dots in the solid-state is found low and needs to increase it. Recently, researchers are trying to improve the solid-state photoluminescence efficiency of carbon quantum dots by introducing aggregation induced emission (AIE) without composite formation [34–37] and pressure induced luminescence enhancement [38].

In last few years, carbon quantum dots are tremendously used for sensing of different metal ions, molecules, and especially different types of nitro explosives [39–44]. CQDs used to detect nitroexplosives by fluorescence quenching or colorimetric methods [45–47]. Carbon quantum dots are comprehensively used for selective detection of nitro explosives. But there are few report published on selective detection of more than one nitro explosive [48,49]. 2, 4-dinitro aniline (DNA) and 2, 4-dinitro phenol (DNP) both are toxic and explosive materials and these materials are also used for preparation of azo dye, sulphur dye and organic pigments. Contamination of these materials makes serious effects on human bodies and environment. Till date, some works are reported for the detection of 2, 4-dinitro phenol using carbon dots but detection of 2,4-dinitro aniline using carbon dots are still not reported. Therefore, selective detection of both DNP and DNA using carbon quantum dots is an interesting area of research.

In this work, we have tuned solubility of carbon quantum dots in different kinds of solvents by surface functionalization technique. In this technique, we have synthesized three sets of alkyl amine functionalized CQDs (methyl amine, n-butyl amine and n-octyl amine) from citric acid and certain type of alkyl amine by solvothermal method. Here, we have observed that the solubility of carbon quantum dots increases from aqueous medium to organic medium with increasing hydrophobic alkyl chain length at the surface of quantum dots. It is seen that methyl amine functionalized CQDs are soluble in aqueous polar medium and octyl amine functionalized CQDs are soluble only in organic medium. However, n-butyl amine functionalized CQDs (C4-CQD) are soluble in any type of aqueous and organic solvents due to presence of both hydrophilic (-OH, -COOH) and hydrophobic (n-butyl) surface groups. Here we have also observed that the C4-CQD shows aggregation induced emission instead of aggregation caused quenching in solid state and shows bright yellow luminescence color upon excitation. Moreover, to the best of our knowledge here we report for the first time, detection of both dinitroaniline (DNA) and dinitrophenol (DNP) using this C4-CQD via fluorescence quenching and colorimetric methods. We can selectively identify the DNA and DNP using these carbon quantum dots by changing the detection techniques. In fluorescence quenching method, in aqueous medium, only DNP shows significant quenching but in organic medium both shows significant quenching in which emission peak of carbon quantum dots is red shifted (∼30 nm) in presence of DNP whereas any significant peak shifting is not observed for DNA. Here, we have also explained the quenching mechanism based on zeta potential and TCSPC data. Moreover, CQD containing paper strip shows yellow spot after addition of DNP whereas no such characteristic color is appeared after addition of DNA. Based on these two techniques we can selectively identify the DNA and DNP among other nitro compounds using our as-synthesized carbon quantum dots.

Citric acid, methyl amine, n-butyl amine, n-octyl amine, ethanol, nitro benzene (NB), 4-nitro benzoic acid (NBA), 1, 3-dinitrobenzene (DNB), 2, 4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), 2, 4-dinitrophenol (DNP), 2, 4- dinitroaniline (DNA), 2- nitrophenol (NP), 2- nitrotoluene (NT), 3-nitroaniline (NA), acetone, ethyl acetate, dichloromethane (DCM), chloroform, toluene, benzene, n-hexane, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), 1,4-Dioxane, tetrahydrofuran (THF) were purchased from Merck India. 1 kDa dialysis bags were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All reagents were analytically grade and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of alkyl amine functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: We have synthesized three sets of carbon quantum dots with methyl amine, n-butyl amine and n-octyl amine functionalization by simple one step solvothermal method and the samples are designed as C1-CQD, C4-CQD and C8-CQD. C1-CQDs are synthesized from citric acid and methyl amine. In this method, 0.5 g of citric acid is dissolved in 20 mL of milli-Q-water and then 140 μL (0.00303 mol) of methyl amine is added to it and stirred for 2 h. The colourless solution is then transferred into 50 mL Teflon equipped stainless steel auto clave and kept it at 180 °C for 10 h. After completion of reaction, the brown liquid is filtered through 0.05 μm Millipore filter paper. The filtrate solution is then dialysed through 1 kDa dialysis bag for 48 h to remove all un-reacted molecules.
To synthesize C4-CQDs, 300 μL (0.00303 mol) of n-butyl amine and 0.5 g citric acid are mixed in 20 mL of ethanol and followed the same hydrothermal process under similar reaction condition.

To synthesize C8-CQDs, 500 μL (0.00303 mol) of n-octyl amine and 0.5 g citric acids are dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol and stirred for 2 h and followed the same procedure as other sample. All the carbon quantum dots were kept in refrigerator.

To understand the morphology of quantum dots, JEOL-2011 instrument is used to record Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images of all functionalized carbon quantum dots. TEM is operated at 200 kV and fitted with Gathan CCD camera. For TEM analysis, carbon quantum dots are dispersed in acetone and drop cast on the 300 mesh carbon coated Cu grid. NICOLET MAGNA IR 750 system is used to record Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR). Solid carbon quantum dots are mixed with dried KBr powder and make a transparent pellet by applying hydraulic pressure for FT-IR analysis and the IR spectra are recorded from 400 to 4000 cm-1. X-Ray Photoelectron spectra (XPS) are taken from OMICRON-0571 system having Al-Kα monochromatic X-ray source with excitation 1486.7 eV at power of 300 W. For XPS analysis we have prepared a thin film of CQDs on silicon wafer. Zeta-potentials of all functionalized CQDs are performed in ethanol medium using Malvern instrument. UV-VIS absorption spectra of CQDs are taken from Cary UV 5000 spectrophotometer having Tungsten halogen visible and deuterium arc UV source. PTI Fluromax QM-400 spectrofluorometer are used to record photoluminescence spectra. Spectrofluorometer have xenon lamp source and photoluminescence emission spectra of all samples are taken from different excitation wavelength (300 nm–400 nm) at integration time of 0.05s and slit width 0.12 mm. HORIBA Scientific DeltaFlex modular fluorescence lifetime system is used to perform the Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) of CQDs in presence and absence of nitro compounds using a 375 nm ps diode Laser. The decay was recorded for CQD at 375 nm excitation wavelength and at 450 nm emission wavelength. CQD and in presence of DNA and DNP are fitted to tri exponential function, I = A1exp (-t/τ1) + A2exp (-t/τ2) + A3exp (-t/τ3). Average life time is calculated by the following equation:

Where τ1, τ2, τ3 are the luminescence decay life times and A1, A2, A3 are the weighing parameter.

Morphology of as-synthesized carbon quantum dots was investigated by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The HR-TEM image of three different CQDs C1-CQD, C4-CQD and C8-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 a, 1(b) and 1(c) and it is seen that the quantum dots are nearly spherical, uniformly dispersed with diameter varies from 2.0 nm to 4.0 nm. The calculated the average size of carbon quantum dots from the Gaussian fitted size distribution curve for C1-CQD, C4-CQD and C8-CQD are found to 2.6 ± 0.06, 2.5 ± 0.04, and 3.0 ± 0.07 nm respectively. The Gaussian distribution is shown in the inset of images. From the TEM images it is seen that as-synthesized C1-CQD, C4-CQD and C8-CQDs are almost similar in size.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was carried out to understand the surface functional groups of carbon quantum dots. Fig. 1d shows the FT-IR spectra of all three carbon quantum dots. A broad peak in the range of 3140–3580 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of –O-H and –N-H bonds. The peaks appeared at 2926 cm−1 and 2858 cm−1 due to –C-H stretching vibration [50] and it is also noticed that the intensity of this peak increased from C1-CQD to C8-CQD due to increase in the methylene (–CH2) group at the surface of carbon quantum dots which indicates that the functionalization occurs by long chain alkyl group (n-butyl, n-octyl). A strong peak in the range of 1690–1705 cm−1 attributed to the –C=O stretching vibration of acid functional groups [51]. Peaks at 1190 cm−1 and 1129 cm−1 correspond to –C-N and –C-O stretching vibrations [52,53]. Peak at the lower wavelength at around 670 cm−1 appears due to –N-H wagging.

Values of Zeta potentials of C1-CQD, C4-CQD and C8-CQDs are found to be −7.44, −7.80 and −7.09 mV respectively as shown in Fig. S1 to Fig. S3. It is observed that all CQDs show negative zeta potentials which imply that the quantum dots have electron rich surface functional groups (-OH, -COOH, -NHR) [54]. Almost similar values of zeta potentials also indicate that nearly same percentages of amine group are functionalized in all the samples.

X-Ray Photoelectron spectroscopy measurements were carried out to understand the chemical composition of carbon quantum dots. The full range XPS spectra of C1-CQD, C4-CQD and C8-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 a, Fig. 2b and c which consist of three peaks for three different elements of C1s (284.1 eV), N1s (400.0 eV) and O1s (531.0 eV). We have calculated the atomic percentages of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen in CQDs from these spectra and it is seen that the value of the atomic percentage of N is almost same for all the carbon dots which suggests that nearly same amount of alkyl amine are attached with quantum dots. Fig. 2d, e and f show the high resolution de-convoluted spectra of C1s for C1-CQD, C4-CQD and C8-CQDs respectively. It is seen that these peaks are fitted with three peaks at around 284.6 eV, 285.5 eV, and 287.8 eV respectively which correspond to C=C/C-C, C-N/C-O, and C=O bonds respectively. The high resolution de-convoluted N1s spectra of all three CQDs are shown in Fig. 2g, h and i. The N1s spectra exhibit three different peaks at around 398.5 eV, 399.2 eV, and 400.0 eV corresponds to pyridinic ‘N’, amino ‘N’ and pyrrolic ‘N’ groups respectively. We have also calculated the percentage of these three elements for all three carbon quantum dots using Scofield's relative sensitivity factor and it is shown in Table-1 in supporting information. It is seen that percentage of amino N and pyridinic N are decreases and pyrrolic N increases in carbon quantum dots with increasing the hydrophobic chain length of amine. Fig. 2j, k and l show the high resolution de-convoluted spectra of O1s for C1-CQD, C4-CQD and C8-CQDs respectively which consist of two peaks for C=O and C-OH bond at 531.7eV and 532.4 eV respectively [55].
Therefore, we confirmed the formation of alkyl amine functionalized carbon quantum dots from the analysis of the above characterization data.

The as-synthesized carbon dots C1-CQD, C4-CQD and C8-CQD show excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence behavior as shown in Fig. 3 . The excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence spectra are appeared from different defect states which arise due to surface functionalization [56]. Based on the luminescence properties of carbon quantum dots, we have investigated the solubility of the as synthesized CQDs in different solvent media. It is seen that C1-CQD only soluble in polar solvent like water and shows bright blue luminescence under UV-light but doesn't exhibit any luminescence in non-polar solvent like toluene due to presence of large number of electron rich hydrophilic functional groups (-OH, -NH2, -COOH) at the surface of quantum dots. This C1-CQD shows excitation wavelength dependent PL with highest emission peak centered at 453 nm when excited at 380 nm wavelength as shown in Fig. 3a. Similarly, C4-CQDs are soluble in both polar and non-polar solvents and show blue luminescence color under UV-irradiation. The excitation wavelength dependent PL spectra of C4-CQD in water and toluene are shown in Fig. 3b and c. Whereas the as-synthesized C8-CQDs are highly soluble in non-polar solvent like toluene, benzene, hexane but insoluble in water and shows blue luminescence under UV-light but doesn't show any luminescence in water medium. The C8-CQDs in toluene solvent exhibit excitation wavelength dependent PL spectra with intense emission peak centered at 456 nm when excited at 380 nm as shown in Fig. 3d.

In this report, solubility of carbon quantum dots is tuned by surface functionalization using alkyl amine (methyl-, n-butyl and n-octyl amine). We have checked the solubility of carbon quantum dots in different types of solvents using the highly luminescent properties of CQDs. We have prepared three sets of carbon quantum dots functionalized by methyl amine (C1-CQD), butyl amine (C4-CQD) and octyl amine (C8-CQD) respectively and these solid CQDs are added in different types of solvent containing 0.5 mg/mL of CQDs and stirred for few minutes. Then the solution is taken under the UV lamp (λ 365nm) to check the luminescence behavior of solution. It is observed that the solubility of the as-synthesized CQDs in non-polar organic solvent increases as well as the solubility in polar solvent decreases with increasing the chain length of hydrophobic alkyl group at the surface of quantum dots. The C1-CQDs in water show blue luminescence but there is no luminescent color noticed in toluene solution, similarly, C4-CQD solution shows bright blue luminescence color in both water and toluene but for the C8-CQDs in toluene show blue luminescence but there is no luminescent color noticed in water under UV-irradiation. The results are shown in Fig. 3e. It is also noticed that C4-CQDs in different types of solvents such as polar protic, polar aprotic and non-polar solvents show bright blue luminescence color due to excellent solubility in all types of solvents as shown in Fig. 4 a. C4-CQD in different type solvent solutions shows similar excitation wavelength dependent PL spectra. The Excitation wavelength dependent PL spectra of carbon quantum dots in different solvents are shown in Fig. S4 to Fig. S9 in supporting information.

We have also checked the solubility of CQDs by liquid-liquid extraction method. In this method, 5 mL water containing C1-CQDs are taken in sample vial, and 5 mL of toluene are added to this solution. These two liquids are immiscible which is clearly observed and only water solution is blue luminescent under UV light. Next the vial is shaken and settled for few minutes under UV light and it is seen that initially total mixture shows luminescence but after some time the solvents get separated from each other and only water part shows luminescence as carbon quantum dots are not soluble in toluene medium. Similar experiment we have carried out using water containing C4-CQDs and pure toluene. Both solvents show blue luminescence but the intensity of water solution decreased after shaking because some carbon dots move from water to toluene. For C8-CQD, 5 mL of toluene containing C8-CQDs are taken in sample vial, and 5 mL of water are added to this solution. After shaking the whole mixture, it shows luminescence but after phase separation of solvent, only toluene part shows luminescence as C8-CQDs do not soluble in water. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 4b–(d) and video 1 to 3 in supporting information.

We have studied the concentration dependent and solid-state photoluminescence spectra for C4-CQD. We have taken photoluminescence spectra of C4-CQD aqueous solution at different concentrations of 0.6, 1.25, 2.5, 5.0 and 10 mg/mL. It is found that all samples show excitation wavelength dependent PL as shown in Figs. S10–S14 in the supporting information. From these PL spectra, small red shifting in emission peak is observed with increasing concentration of CQDs in solution. The emission peak is shifted from 442 nm to 480 nm with increasing concentration of CQDs from 0.6 to 10 mg/mL as shown in Fig. 5 a. The digital image of luminescent carbon quantum dots at different concentrations from 0.6 to 10 mg/mL under UV-lamp is shown in Fig. 5b. In previous report, the PL emission peak is significantly red shifted with increasing concentration and shows blue to green or red luminescence with increasing concentration of carbon dots [57,58]. However, in our case, both hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups are present at the surface of quantum dots. The hydrophilic groups (-OH, -COOH) are prone to aggregate with increasing concentration through H-bonding but the presence of hydrophobic alkyl group at the surface of carbon quantum dots decreases this aggregation significantly, and as a result there is small red shift is observed in emission peak. CQDs generally lose their luminescence nature in solid state film or powder form due to aggregation caused quenching and this is happened because of π– π stacking or excessive non-radiative energy transfer [28]. Interestingly, our as-synthesized C4-CQD in solid state shows yellow luminescence under UV–irradiation and exhibits excitation wavelength independent PL spectra. Solid state PL spectra of C4-CQD and luminescent color are shown in Fig. 5c. The PL emission peak of C4-CQD is red shifted from 450 nm to 530 nm with a shoulder peak at 600 nm in the solution state solid state respectively. In the solid state, the surface n-butyl group of CQDs prevents the π – π stacking of carbon quantum dots resulting suppression of the aggregation caused quenching. However, there is a possibility of aggression of CQDs in the solid state due to formation of H-bonding between the oxygen and nitrogen containing surface functional groups [59]. The π – π electronic conjugation increases due to the aggregation and as a result solid carbon quantum dots show yellow luminescence [60,61]. The individual carbon quantum dots have different degrees of defect states due to functionalization and doping of oxygen or nitrogen atoms and emission of excited electrons from these different defects state to the ground state (valence band) creates excitation wavelength dependent PL spectra for the C4-CQDs in solution state. But the individual nature of quantum dots is lost after the aggregation as result excitation independent PL spectra is found and emission peak is red shifted. As the C4-CQD shows solid state luminescence, this C4-CQD could be useful for fingerprint identification, light-emitting diode applications.

The as-synthesized C4-CQDs are used for the selective detection of 2, 4-dinitroaniline (DNA) and 2, 4-dinitrophenol (DNP). DNP is highly soluble in water and DNA is practically insoluble in water but both are highly soluble in organic solvent like acetone, THF, 1, 4-dioxane. As the C4-CQDs are also soluble both in aqueous and organic solvent, we can selectively detect DNA and DNP by changing the solvent medium. In these experiments, we have performed photoluminescence quenching experiment in water and 1, 4-dioxane medium after gradual addition of different nitro aromatics e.g. DNP, DNA, DNT, DNB, NP, NA, NT, NBA, NB in C4-CQD solution.

Firstly, we have performed this quenching experiment in aqueous medium. In this titration experiment, 3 mL of aqueous C4-CQD (0.5 mg/mL) solution is taken in a quartz cell and recorded PL emission spectra for carbon quantum dots at 380 nm excitation wavelength. After gradual addition of 10 μL of 0.5 mM DNP solution PL emission spectra of this mixture are also recorded. It is seen that 78% PL quenching occurs after addition of 50 μM of DNP solution into the C4-CQD solution as shown in Fig. 6 a. Similarly we have investigated the nature of the PL quenching in presence of same amount of DNA in C4-CQD solution and it is seen that only 26% PL quenching occurs in presence of 50 μM of DNA as shown in Fig. 6b. We have also added same amount of other nitro compounds individually into the carbon quantum dots solution and recorded PL emission spectra. From the experimental data, it is observed that in aqueous medium only DNP shows significant PL quenching compared to other nitro compounds including DNA due to very low solubility in water. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 6c.

As all nitro compounds and our as-synthesized C4-CQD are soluble in organic solvent such as acetone, THF, 1,4-dioxane; we have performed the same quenching experiments in 1,4-dioxane medium instead of aqueous medium to get more selective results. It is seen that DNP and DNA both are shows significant PL quenching behavior and 94% and 82% PL quenching are observed in presence of 58 μM concentrations of DNP and DNA respectively. It is also noticed that after gradual addition of DNP solution, the PL emission peak is red shifted to ∼30 nm and this shift of the emission peak is not prominent at higher concentration of DNP (48–58 μM). But no shift in the emission peak is observed after gradual addition of DNA, although at higher concentration (40–58 μM), the emission peak slightly red shifted to ∼ 5 nm. This significant red-shifting in presence of DNP occurs be due to energy transfer or complex formation of DNP with surface group of carbon dots [62,63]. The PL quenching spectra in presence of DNA and DNP are shown in Fig. 7 a and b. In both DNP and DNA have two electron with drawing nitro group (-NO2) due to presence of the –NO2 group, hydrogen atom of –NH2 group in DNA and hydrogen atom of –OH in DNP both are acidic in nature. Moreover, oxygen is more electronegative than nitrogen atom, so, bond polarity in H-O is high than in H-N, as a result H-atom of –OH in DNP is more acidic than DNA molecule. Therefore, DNP molecule easily form complex through H-atoms with electron rich surface functional group of carbon quantum dots as compared to DNA. This also supports from the zeta potential measurements. The zeta potential of pure C4-CQD and in presence of DNA and DNP individually are −7.80, −6.65 and −5.0 mV respectively as shown in Fig. S15 and Fig. S16 in supporting information. It is seen that in presence of DNP zeta potential value significantly change which conclude that there is electrostatic interaction between DNP and carbon dots [64]. PL quenching efficiency in presence of other nitro compounds also investigated and it is seen that except DNA and DNP, other nitro compounds doesn't show significant PL quenching efficiency in presence of same amount of nitro compounds. The results are as shown in Fig. 8 a.
PL quenching efficiency also studied by Stern-Volmer plot using Stern-Volmer equation.

I0/I = Ksv [Q] + 1, where I and I0 are fluorescence intensities in presence and absence of nitro compounds, [Q] is molar concentration of nitro compounds, Ksv is quenching constant. Fig. 7c and d show the S-V plot of DNA and DNP respectively. The S-V plots of other nitro compounds are shown in Fig. S17 to Fig. S23 in supporting information. From these plots, it is seen that for DNA, the curve is linear at lower concentration but with increasing concentration the linearity gets deviated and follows exponentially but for DNP, the curve is fitted with linear equation throughout the whole range. Similarly other nitro compounds also show linear S-V curve. We have calculated quenching constant, Ksv values for all nitro compounds and it is found that DNA and DNP both show higher quenching constant values as compared to other nitro compounds. The values of Ksv for all nitro compounds are shown in Table-2 in the supporting information. Similarly, we have also studied the quenching efficiencies of different metal ions in presence of same concentration. But significant PL quenching is not observed in presence of these metal ions. The experimental PL quenching result are shown in Fig. S24 to Fig. S31 in supporting information.

We have performed similar PL quenching experiment to investigate the selectivity of CQDs towards the detection of DNA and DNP in presence of other nitro compounds. Initially, we have examined the selectivity for the detection of DNA and DNP in presence of other single individual nitro compounds. For this experiment, 3 mL of CQDs solution is taken in a quartz cell and PL emission spectra are recorded. Next, 80 μL of 2 mM DNB is mixed with this quantum dots solution and emission spectra are measured. In the next step, 80 μL of 2 mM DNP is added into this mixture and again the emission spectra are recorded. We have performed the same experiment with other nitro compounds followed by addition of DNP. We have also carried out similar titration experiment using DNA instead of DNP. So, the selectivity of CQDs towards DNA and DNP in presence of individual other nitro compounds are shown in Fig. 8b and c. It is found that the CQDs show high selectivity for detection of DNA and DNP. We have also examined the selectivity for detection of DNA and DNP in presence of multiple nitro compounds. To perform this titration experiment, In the first step, PL emission spectra of pure carbon dots in organic solvent are taken and then mixture of 10 μL of 1 mM nitro compounds (NBA, DNB, DNT, NA, NT etc.) are added. Finally, same amount of DNA or DNP solutions are added to this CQDs solution. Emission spectra of carbon quantum dots solution are taken after every step of mixing. To check the selectivity, we have performed the same experiment for four consecutive times. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 8d and e. It is seen that negligible PL intensity change after addition of the mixture of nitro compounds but significant PL quenching is observed in presence of same amount of DNP or DNA. Moreover, besides this significant PL quenching, red shifting of emission peak is also noticed after DNP addition. We have also checked the selectivity for detection of DNA in presence of DNP using the similar titration experiment. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 8f. In this case, PL peaks are quenched significantly after each step of addition of DNA and DNP solutions respectively. But PL emission peaks are clearly red shifted by ∼10 nm after each addition of DNP, whereas the position of PL emission peaks remains unchanged after addition of DNA. Therefore, from these titration experiments it is concluded that individually DNP and DNA can be selectively detected in presence of other nitro compounds using C4-CQDs.

In the literature there are several quenching mechanisms are reported for sensing of nitro explosives e.g. fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism (FRET), electron transfer mechanism, static quenching, dynamic quenching mechanism etc. PL quenching occurs in presence of nitro compounds due to mainly static and dynamic quenching mechanism. In static quenching, the fluorescent carbon quantum dot forms a non-fluorescent complex with quencher. But in dynamic quenching, fluorescence is quenched when the collision occurs between excited molecules of fluorophore (CQDs) and the quencher (nitro compounds) or any non-radiative energy transfer occurs. Due to presence of electron withdrawing nitro group, nitro aromatics are highly electron deficient and our as-synthesized carbon quantum dots are electron rich as confirmed from zeta potential value. So, the CQDs are prone to transfer electron cloud from electron rich carbon quantum dots to electron deficient nitro compounds and as a result PL quenching occurs and the quenching efficiency increases for high electron deficient nitro compounds. Therefore, the quenching efficiencies for dinitro compounds (DNA, DNP, DNT, DNB) are expected to be higher than the mono substituted nitro compounds (NT, NA, NB, NP, NBA). But in our case DNT and DNB show very low quenching efficiency even lower than NA and NP. So, the electron transfer is not the only factor for PL quenching rather another predominating factor is the energy transfer e.g. FRET and it is applicable if the emission spectra of donor fluorophore overlap with the absorbance spectra of acceptor molecule (nitro compounds). The quenching efficiency is directly proportional to the overlap integral of emission and absorption spectra. Here we have checked this possibility and it is seen that emission spectra of the as-synthesized carbon quantum dots effectively overlap with the absorbance spectra of DNP and DNA as shown in Fig. 9 a and b. We have also calculated the overlap integral [J(λ)] for all the nitro compounds using the following equation:
(i)

( λ )

Where, F (λ) is the fluorescence intensity of the donor (carbon dot) in the range of λ to λ +Δλ and
ε A
is the extinction coefficient of the acceptor (nitro compounds).

The calculated values of overlap integral for all nitro compounds are shown in Table 3 in the supporting information. It is seen that DNA and DNP show high overlap integral compare to other nitro compounds suggesting that the FRET mechanism is being operated in this case. Another contributing factor i.e. inner filter effects (IFE) is operated here as the excitation spectra of carbon quantum dots effectively overlap with the absorbance spectra of nitro compounds. The excitation and emission overlap spectrum with different nitro compounds are shown in Fig. S32 in supporting information.

We have further investigated PL quenching mechanism using stern-volmer plot. It is seen that DNP shows linear S-V plot whereas DNA shows exponential behavior. In lower concentration region DNA shows linear curve however with increasing concentration it gets deviated from linearity and exponential behavior is observed due to both dynamic (FRET) and static quenching [65]. But for DNP, the S-V curve is fitted with liner equation which represents only static quenching occurs in this case. To confirm this, we have further investigated the time-correlated single-photon counting measurements to measure the fluorescence life time of carbon quantum dots in presence and absence of different concentration of DNA and DNP. The fluorescence life times of CQDs and also in presence of 10, 20, 40, 60 μM DNA are 8.99, 8.69, 8.32, 7.21 and 6.67 ns respectively. The fluorescence life times of carbon quantum dots and in presence of same concentration of DNP are 8.86, 8.78, 8.82, 8.68, 8.56 ns. The fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs and in presence of DNA and DNP are shown in Fig. 9c and d. We have further studied the τ0/τ vs. concentration of DNA and DNP plot as shown in Fig. 9e. A horizontal straight line is fitted for DNP whereas for DNA a linear straight line is observed suggesting dynamic quenching occurs in presence of DNA. The fluorescence life time of CQDs and in presence of DNP do not change significantly and τ0/τ vs. DNP curve fitted with horizontal straight line implies that the PL quenching occurs due to static quenching. But changing of fluorescence lifetime in presence of DNA over rules the inner filter effect (IFE). Small decrease in fluorescence life time in presence of DNA and small red shift (∼5 nm) in emission peak is observed in presence of high concentration of DNA suggesting that the quenching occurs due to both dynamic (FRET) and static quenching [66].

Therefore, from the above analysis of quenching constant, overlap integral values and fluorescence life time, it is concluded that PL quenching of CQDs occurs in presence of DNP due to static quenching whereas for DNA, the PL quenching is guided by both dynamic quenching and static quenching mechanism. The schematic diagram of PL quenching mechanism is shown in Fig. 9f.

We have also observed a yellow color appeared after addition of DNP in carbon quantum dots contained 1, 4-dioxane solution. Therefore, we have performed the colorimetric titration experiments in organic medium. In this experiment, we have observed the color change in day light in presence of different amounts (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 10, 13 and 16 μM) of DNP in carbon quantum dots solution. It is seen that with increasing the amount of DNP a yellow color is appeared. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 10 a. We have also performed the same experiment in absence of carbon quantum dots where no such yellow color is observed in presence of same amount of DNP in 1,4-dioxane as shown in Fig. S33 in the supporting information. We have also performed same titration experiments using DNA solution but no characteristic color is observed in presence of the same amount of DNA. The results are shown in Fig. S34 in supporting information. To confirm the selectivity for the detection of DNP using this method, we have added same amount of different nitro compounds in the carbon dots solution. It is seen that in presence of 16 μM of different nitro compounds only DNP shows characteristic yellow color as shown in Fig. 10b. This characteristic yellow color is appeared due to complex formation with carbon quantum dots. We have further investigated this observation using UV–Vis spectroscopy. We have recorded UV–Vis spectra of pure CQD in 1, 4-dioxane medium and after gradual addition of DNP or DNA. Pure C4-CQD shows two absorption peaks at 275 nm and 350 nm corresponding to π-π* and n-π* transition. After gradual addition of DNP, the 275 nm peak remains unchanged but 350 nm peak is red shifted and in presence of 16 μM DNP absorption the peaks are appeared at 362 nm with a small hump at 417 nm resulting appearance of yellow colored solution. In case of DNA, after addition of 16 μM DNA the absorption peaks is appeared at 330 nm. The UV–Vis experimental results after addition of DNP and DNA in CQD solution is shown in Fig. 11 a and b.

For practical applications, the as-synthesized n-butyl amine functionalized carbon quantum dots are soaked in a non-fluorescent filter paper strip and then dried. The dried carbon quantum dots containing paper strip shows blue luminescence under UV-lamp and white in day light. Next 5 μL of 500 μM DNP solution is dropped on the paper strip and dried. After drying a yellow spot is observed in day light and under UV-lamp a non-fluorescent spot is observed in blue luminescent paper strip as shown in Fig. 11c. Similar experiment has been carried out using DNA and it is observed that the dry paper strip does not show any significant color after DNA addition but a non-fluorescent spot is appeared in luminescent paper strip under UV-lamp as shown in Fig. 11d. Therefore, we can easily selectively detect DNA and DNP using this C4-CQD containing paper strip.

In summary, we have tuned the solubility of carbon quantum dots by alkyl amine functionalization using simple solvothermal method. Solubility of carbon quantum dots increases from aqueous solvent to organic solvents with increasing chain length of alkyl amine at the surface of carbon quantum dots. However, butyl amine functionalized carbon quantum dots are soluble in any type of solvent due to presence of both hydrophilic and hydrophobic functional groups at the surface of the quantum dots whereas octyl amine functionalized carbon quantum dots are soluble only in organic solvents. In solid state, butyl amine functionalized carbon quantum dots emit yellow color with excitation wavelength independent PL as the presence of hydrophobic butyl chain at the surface prevents the π-π staking and aggregation caused quenching. Moreover, C4-CQDs are used for selective detection of DNP and DNA by changing solvent medium. In aqueous medium, only DNP shows significant PL quenching whereas in organic solvent medium both DNA and DNP show significant PL quenching. In presence of DNP, emission peak of carbon quantum dots of size ∼30 nm is red shifted whereas in presence of DNA, no peak shifting is observed. C4-CQD containing paper strip shows yellow sport after addition of DNP but no yellow coloration is observed on the paper in presence of DNA. Here, we have been able to detect DNA and DNP selectively using both fluorescence quenching and colorimetric methods with high selectivity.
